docs.title: katastrophe
docs.text:
A small, local sandbox for running untrusted code — LLM agents, random PyPI packages, model weights — on your Linux machine without giving it reach to your home directory, SSH keys, VPN/tailnet, or other machines.

The agent runs in a throwaway qemu/KVM VM (or a rootless-podman container for GPU work). All its network egress goes through a single TLS-terminating proxy with a hostname allowlist; LLM API calls additionally route through a mediator that keeps your real API keys out of the sandbox and runs a probe over every request/response.

It's a few hundred lines of bash + Nix + a thin Rust CLI. Less code is a security feature.

  • Get started

    Build and run the zero-setup appliance in minutes.

    Installation

  • Understand the boundaries

    Where each control sits and what it actually defends.

    Threat model

  • Prove it works

    Runnable checks for every profile and security invariant.

    Testing

  • Look under the hood

    The components, the CLI, and every config knob.

    Components

docs.title: The three profiles
docs.text:
katastrophe is a small kit of components wired into three pre-built recipes. They share the same VM/container containment; they differ in where egress is enforced and therefore in how much host setup they need.

Profile Host setup Egress enforced Best for Appliance (default) none — just qemu + KVM inside the VM (in-guest proxy + nft) trying it out, sharing, no API keys Hardened VM (Linux) bridge + host nftables + proxies host kernel (survives guest compromise) daily use, API-key isolation GPU (Linux) + rootless podman + CDI host kernel (skuid drop on uid 1001) GPU workloads

The appliance moves the proxy + allowlist inside the disposable guest, so it needs no root, no bridge, no host firewall — at the cost that the allowlist is defeatable by a guest-root exploit (which still can't escape the VM). The hardened paths enforce egress in the host kernel, so the gate survives a full guest compromise. See Profiles for the details and Threat model for the trade-offs.

docs.title: Two ways to drive it
docs.text:
• The kata CLI (Rust) — kata appliance, kata up, kata vm, kata doctor, kata dash, … A thin orchestrator over the bash scripts.
  • The bash scripts directly — every kata <x> has a bash scripts/<x>.sh equivalent, with no extra dependencies.

See the kata CLI reference for the full command list.

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• New here? → Appliance install (no host setup).
  • Setting up the daily-driver profile? → Hardened setup.
  • Want to verify the security claims yourself? → Testing.

docs.title: Components & recipes
docs.text: katastrophe is a small kit of sandbox/egress components you can run and test on their own. The named profiles (appliance / hardened-vm / gpu) are just pre-wired recipes — points in a small space, not separate products.

docs.title: The components
docs.text:
Component What it is Run it alone Isolator: VM throwaway qemu/KVM VM + workspace share scripts/appliance.sh Isolator: container rootless podman sandbox (+GPU via CDI) scripts/appliance-container.sh Egress proxy mitmproxy + hostname allowlist scripts/proxy.sh Probe probe(messages, response, meta) -> Verdict examples/probe-over-sample.py Netgate (hardened) host nft skuid drop + bridge + litellm scripts/net-up.sh (host integration, not a drop-in)

The first four are genuinely standalone and rootless. The netgate is a host integration (it edits your nftables); read it for ideas rather than expecting to import it.

docs.title: What combines with what (the constraints)
docs.text:
It's a constrained lattice, not a free grid:

  • GPU ⟹ container. Passing a single laptop GPU into a VM needs VFIO + a spare GPU; not practical. GPU work goes through the container isolator.
  • probe over bodies ⟹ terminating proxy. A passthrough proxy only sees the CONNECT host / SNI; it can't read request/response bodies.
  • terminating proxy ⟹ the client must trust the CA. Passthrough needs no CA anywhere (the appliance's whole point); terminating mode generates one and the isolator must trust it.
  • host-kernel egress ⟹ root + Linux + the nft plumbing, and pairs with the container (pasta carries the host uid → skuid-filterable) or a bridged VM. A rootless SLIRP VM can't be skuid-filtered, so it enforces egress in-guest.

docs.title: Egress enforcement strength
docs.text:
Path Enforced where Survives a sandbox compromise? VM appliance in-guest proxy + nft no (guest root can disable), but can't escape the VM hardened (vm/gpu) host kernel (nft skuid drop) yes container appliance + proxy.sh proxy env (advisory) no — the workload can unset HTTP_PROXY

So appliance-container.sh trades isolation strength for speed and zero setup. For a real egress boundary, use the VM appliance (in-guest) or the hardened path (host kernel).

docs.title: Recipes (the named profiles)
docs.text: Recipe = Command appliance VM + in-guest passthrough kata appliance --workspace DIR hardened vm VM + host netgate + terminating proxy + probe kata up then kata vm --workspace DIR gpu container + host netgate + terminating proxy + probe kata up then kata gpu --workspace DIR

docs.title: Demos
docs.text:
All rootless, no host setup. Build the container image once: bash scripts/build-appliance-container.sh.

Probe alone (no infra):

python examples/probe-over-sample.py

Proxy alone (point any client at it):

bash scripts/proxy.sh                 # passthrough on 127.0.0.1:8080
# elsewhere:
HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:8080 curl https://pypi.org/      # allowed
HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:8080 curl https://example.com/   # 403, not allowed

Container sandbox alone:

mkdir -p /tmp/proj
bash scripts/appliance-container.sh --workspace /tmp/proj            # open egress
bash scripts/appliance-container.sh --workspace /tmp/proj --no-int   # offline

Proxy in front of the container sandbox (the composition):

bash examples/proxy-in-front.sh --workspace /tmp/proj            # passthrough
bash examples/proxy-in-front.sh --workspace /tmp/proj --probe    # terminate + probe (experimental)

Inside the container:

curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/     # allowed
curl -sS https://example.com/                                   # denied

Edit the allowlist in proxy/mitmproxy/allowlist.py.

docs.title: Profiles
docs.text:
The three named profiles aren't separate products — they're pre-wired recipes over the same small set of components. They share one mental model: three independent pillars, each doing one job.

  1. Network filter — the mitmproxy allowlist + the agent_isolate nft skuid drop. Governs what gets out. Conceptually the same on every path.
  2. The VM (qemu/KVM) — containment for non-GPU work. The host does the I/O and maps ownership across the boundary, so there's no uid dance.
  3. The GPU container (rootless podman, host uid 1001) — the only path that runs host-side as 1001 and persists work on a 1001-owned tree.

docs.title: Escape-landing calibration
docs.text:
Where a real runtime escape lands — not guest-root, which stays trapped behind qemu and touches nothing on the host:

Profile Outer wall Runs as Escape lands as Caught after by GPU (rootless podman) namespaces (weakest) agent 1001 agent 1001 agent_isolate + 0700 home + 755 source Appliance (rootless qemu) KVM (strong) you (1000) you (1000) host kernel only (no host nft) Hardened VM (sudo vm.sh) KVM (strong) agent (§5d drop) agent 1001 agent_isolate skuid drop

Weak wall ↔ punishing landing; strong wall ↔ soft landing. Coherent: the appliance trades the host gate away for zero setup, so it leans entirely on KVM.

docs.title: The constraint lattice
docs.text:
It's a constrained space, not a free grid:

  • GPU ⟹ container. Passing a single laptop GPU into a VM needs VFIO + a spare GPU; not practical. GPU work goes through the container isolator.
  • probe over bodies ⟹ terminating proxy. A passthrough proxy only sees the CONNECT host / SNI; it can't read request/response bodies.
  • terminating proxy ⟹ the client must trust the CA. Passthrough needs no CA anywhere (the appliance's whole point); terminating mode generates one and the isolator must trust it.
  • host-kernel egress ⟹ root + Linux + the nft plumbing, and pairs with the container (pasta carries the host uid → skuid-filterable) or a bridged VM. A rootless SLIRP VM can't be skuid-filtered, so it enforces egress in-guest.

docs.title: Egress enforcement strength (be honest about it)
docs.text:
Path Enforced where Survives a sandbox compromise? VM appliance in-guest proxy + nft no (guest root can disable), but can't escape the VM hardened (vm/gpu) host kernel (nft skuid drop) yes

The appliance trades isolation strength for speed and zero setup. For an egress boundary that survives guest compromise, use the hardened path.

docs.title: The credential routing model
docs.text:
Three categories, treated differently:

  1. OAuth subscription CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini CLI). The SDK does its own auth dance and won't route through litellm. Decision: accept that OAuth creds live inside the sandbox (fixed-cost subscriptions → bounded loss). The mitmproxy allowlist permits the OAuth hosts.
  2. API-key CLIs. Set a fake key + point *_BASE_URL at litellm. litellm holds the real key, validates the fake one, swaps and forwards — the real key never enters the sandbox.
  3. Local (Ollama / llama.cpp). Runs on the host, reached via litellm as a backend. Same probe path as cloud APIs.

See the Threat model for why each landing is acceptable.

docs.title: Storage & sharing work
docs.text: kata disk is an optional reference implementation, not a required step. Adapt it, or skip it and point MOUNT_HOME at a plain directory with the right ownership. The valuable part is the ownership model; the LUKS/USB plumbing is just my choice.

docs.title: The governing rule: trust flows one way
docs.text:
Code/data may flow trusted → sandbox freely (even read-only-shared); the reverse — anything the agent wrote — must never be executed or deserialized as the host user. A model pickle, a trust_remote_code=True repo, even a pip install -e . of an agent-authored tree all run arbitrary code as whoever loads them.

  • you → agent: fine. You feed your own code/models in.
  • agent → you: inspect/read only. The single sanctioned return channel is git fetch — git objects are data, not executed.

docs.title: Two mirror ownership classes
docs.text:
This split is the security model. Anything that protects you from the agent (snapshots, VM bases) lives on a subvolume the agent uid can't write — never nested under the agent-owned @home.

subvol mount owner mode agent snapshot @home /mnt/agent-home agent:agent 755 rw yes @state /mnt/agent-state agent:agent 700 rw yes @shared /mnt/shared-storage you 755 ro no @bases /mnt/vm-bases you 700 none no @snapshots /mnt/snapshots root:root 700 none (holds ro snaps)
  • @home / @state are agent-rw, you-ro — workspace, outputs, sandbox creds. You read + git fetch out; never execute what the agent wrote.
  • @shared / @bases are you-rw, agent-ro/none — heavy read-only inputs (model weights, project-source ingress) and VM bases. @bases/@snapshots out of agent reach is the hardening that stops base/snapshot poisoning (qcow2 parsing is a code-execution surface — see threat K.9b).

docs.title: Disk commands
docs.text:
kata disk status      # inventory + mount state
kata disk init        # one-time: create the subvolumes (destructive on the dev)
kata disk mount       # unlock + mount all + pre-session snapshot of @home/@state
kata disk snap        # on-demand read-only snapshot before a risky run
kata disk rollback    # restore @home/@state from a snapshot
kata disk umount      # revoke GPU ACLs, unmount, close LUKS
kata disk reset       # tear down + clear snapshots

Set AGENT_DEV_UUID (your LUKS partition UUID, from lsblk -o NAME,UUID) in the gitignored config.local.sh first — disk.sh errors loudly if it's unset.

docs.title: Moving a project in and out (git interchange)
docs.text:
A project crosses the boundary only over git. kata project wraps the mechanism:

kata project add  ~/code/myproj [name]   # bare ingress in shared-storage/src/<name>,
                                         # wire remotes, clone into agent work/<name>
kata project push <name>                 # (in your checkout) push your branch → ingress,
                                         # fetch it into the agent worktree
kata project pull <name>                 # fetch the agent's worktree into refs/remotes/agent/*
                                         # — review, never run
kata project list

Topology: the ingress is a you-owned bare repo in shared-storage/src/<name> (not a copy into agent-home), the agent clones it into work/<name> under its own tree, and egress is git fetch back into agent/*. One-way trust is held structurally — there is no shared-writable path bridging the two classes.

docs.title: Sharing a model cache
docs.text:
A shared writable cache the host also loads from is the pickle hole. Two safe shapes:

  • One shared cache, read-only into the sandbox, populated only by you. Put it outside your 0700 home, owned by you, world-readable; mount it --ro. Read-only ⇒ the agent can't poison it.
  • A small agent-writable cache for models only the agent fetches, under @home/.cache/huggingface. Malicious pickles there execute as agent inside the sandbox → contained. Never load from it as yourself.

.safetensors weights carry no code, but config.json, tokenizers, and trust_remote_code .py still execute — don't lean on "safetensors-only" to make a writable shared cache safe.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Pick the profile that matches what you're doing:

  • Appliance install — the zero-host-setup path. Works on any Linux box with KVM, needs no root, no bridge, no firewall, no containers. Best for trying katastrophe out, sharing it, and OAuth-subscription / offline work. Start here.

  • Hardened profile setup — the daily-driver path. Enforces egress in the host kernel (nftables skuid drop), runs a TLS-terminating proxy + an API-key mediator on the host, and adds the GPU container path. Linux/Arch-leaning, needs root for setup.

Both keep the same qemu/KVM containment boundary — they differ only in where the egress allowlist is enforced. If you're not sure, build the appliance first; it's self-contained and the fastest way to see the whole thing working.

Once it runs, head to Testing to verify the egress gate and the security invariants hold on your box.

docs.title: Hardened profile setup
docs.text:
The hardened profiles (VM + GPU) enforce egress in the host kernel, so the allowlist survives a full guest compromise. They need more setup than the appliance: a Linux host, a bridge, host nftables, rootless podman (for GPU), and root for the one-time install.

Read before you run

net-up.sh edits your live nftables ruleset and vm.sh runs qemu via sudo. The host nftables config is assumed to be hand-maintained — cat /etc/nftables.conf and /etc/nftables.d/ first; the nft/*.nft files in the repo are templates, not a stock layout. Review nft/agent_isolate.nft before installing it.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A Linux host with KVM (Intel VT-x / AMD-V), nftables, qemu, virtiofsd, and nix (flakes) for the VM image.
  • For the GPU path: rootless podman + crun, the NVIDIA driver, and CDI (nvidia.com/gpu device) configured.
  • A normal host user agent with uid 1001 (the skuid filter keys off it): 
    sudo useradd -u 1001 -m -s /bin/zsh agent

Run the preflight, which checks all of the above:

kata doctor --hardened

docs.title: 1. Install the persistent egress drop
docs.text:
agent_isolate is the load-bearing host-kernel fail-safe — it drops skuid 1001 → RFC1918 + tailnet + link-local even if every other control fails. It must be loaded persistently (survives reboot); net-up.sh refuses to start without it.

sudo cp nft/agent_isolate.nft /etc/nftables.d/   # or append to /etc/nftables.conf
sudo systemctl enable --now nftables

docs.title: 2. Bring up the session gates + proxies
docs.text:
Once per boot. This creates the bridge, loads the agent_vm / agent_uid_egress tables, generates the per-deployment mitmproxy CA on first run, and starts the mitmproxy + litellm containers.

kata up
kata verify     # confirms both nft tables + both proxy containers are present

kata down tears down the bridge, the session tables (not agent_isolate), and the proxies.

docs.title: 3. Build the VM base image
docs.text:
kata build-base

This builds the NixOS VM qcow2 and bakes the mitmproxy public CA into its trust store. (The GPU container image is built here too, or faster with bash scripts/build-gpu.sh.)

Builds need the egress gate down

kata up pins uid 1001 to the proxy only, so pacman/npm in a container build hit mirror timeouts. Build with kata down, then kata up to run. The build scripts preflight this.

docs.title: 4. Run an agent
docs.text:
VM (non-GPU)GPU (rootless podman) 
# serial console in the foreground:
kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj

# or boot headless + auto-ssh in (better for nvim/TUIs, truecolor):
kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj --ssh

--ssh boots the VM headless in the background and drops you into an ssh session to agent@10.201.0.10 over the bridge; exiting powers the guest off cleanly. Login is agent / agent.

kata gpu --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj

Runs podman run -it directly, so you already get a proper truecolor PTY — no --ssh needed. Egress is pinned to the proxy by agent_uid_egress.

Pass-through flags work the same as the appliance: --mem, --smp, --ro, --rw, --from/--to for warm bases. Read-only --ro mounts may live under your home; the read-write --workspace/--rw may not (a foot-gun guard refuses them).

docs.title: 5. Watch it live (optional)
docs.text:
sudo -v        # prime sudo; dash reads nft + container logs via sudo -n
kata dash

A read-only ratatui TUI: overview health, the mitmproxy egress stream, the litellm + probe stream, and a live nft ruleset viewer.

docs.title: Storage & sharing work
docs.text: For persistent agent storage and the one-way git interchange (moving projects in and out safely), see Storage & sharing. It's optional — point MOUNT_HOME at any directory with the right ownership if you don't want the LUKS/USB layer.

docs.title: Verify it
docs.text: Once a sandbox is running, prove the egress gate and the security invariants hold from inside it: Hardened VM tests and Security invariants.

docs.title: Appliance install (no host setup)
docs.text:
This guide covers the appliance profile — the zero-host-setup path that works on any Linux machine with KVM. It runs the agent in a throwaway qemu/KVM VM with networking and an egress allowlist enforced inside the guest, so it needs no root, no bridge, no host firewall, no containers.

Other profiles

The hardened and GPU profiles (host-enforced egress via nftables + rootless podman) need more host setup and are Linux/Arch-leaning — see Hardened profile setup. This page is the appliance.

Tested on Ubuntu/Debian; package names for Fedora and Arch are noted inline.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• A Linux host with hardware virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) enabled in the BIOS/UEFI.
  • ~4 GB free RAM and ~5 GB disk for the image.
  • Three tools: KVM access, qemu, and nix (to build the image).

That's it. The image is self-contained; everything else (the proxy, the in-guest firewall, the shell) is baked into it at build time.

docs.title: 1. KVM
docs.text:
Check that KVM is available and writable:

ls -l /dev/kvm          # must exist
# optional sanity check:
sudo apt install cpu-checker && kvm-ok

If /dev/kvm exists but you get a permission error later, add yourself to the kvm group and start a new login session:

sudo usermod -aG kvm "$USER"
newgrp kvm              # or just log out and back in

If /dev/kvm is missing, enable virtualization in your BIOS/UEFI.

docs.title: 2. qemu
docs.text:
# Debian / Ubuntu
sudo apt install qemu-system-x86 qemu-utils
# Fedora
sudo dnf install qemu-system-x86 qemu-img
# Arch
sudo pacman -S qemu-base

docs.title: 3. nix (with flakes)
docs.text:
The image is built with Nix flakes. The easiest installer is Determinate Systems' — it sets up the daemon and enables flakes by default:

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf -L https://install.determinate.systems/nix | sh -s -- install

(The upstream installer at https://nixos.org/download works too; the build script passes --extra-experimental-features 'nix-command flakes' so you do not need to edit nix.conf.)

Open a new shell afterwards so nix is on your PATH.

docs.title: 4. Get the code
docs.text:
git clone <repo-url> katastrophe
cd katastrophe

The build must run inside the git clone — it registers a couple of generated files with git add -fN so the flake can see them.

You can drive everything two ways:

  • Directly via the bash scripts (no extra dependencies) — used below.
  • Via the kata CLI (nicer UX, optional). Build it with Rust/cargo: 
    cargo build --release --manifest-path cli/Cargo.toml
export PATH="$PWD/cli/target/release:$PATH"   # so `kata ...` works
    Then every bash scripts/<name>.sh below has a kata <name> equivalent (build-appliance → kata build-appliance, appliance → kata appliance, doctor → kata doctor).

docs.title: 5. Check your host
docs.text:
bash scripts/appliance.sh --help     # or: kata doctor

kata doctor (if you built the CLI) reports KVM + qemu status. The appliance script also preflights these on every run.

docs.title: 6. Build the appliance image
docs.text:
bash scripts/build-appliance.sh      # or: kata build-appliance

First build pulls a lot through Nix and takes a while. The result is a qcow2 under ~/.local/share/katastrophe/appliance-base.qcow2 (no sudo, no /var/lib).

docs.title: 7. Run it
docs.text:
mkdir -p /tmp/proj
bash scripts/appliance.sh --workspace /tmp/proj --ssh
  • --workspace DIR is shared into the guest at /workspace (read-write, over virtio-9p; host file ownership is preserved). It must be outside your home directory — a foot-gun guard refuses paths under $HOME. Use something like /tmp/proj or /srv/work. (To override for a trusted dir: FORBIDDEN_WORKSPACE_PREFIX= bash scripts/appliance.sh --workspace ~/p.)
  • --ro PATH shares an extra directory read-only at /mnt/ro_N (repeatable, up to 4). Unlike --workspace, --ro may live under your home — read-only ingress of a real project is the intended way to feed code in (qemu runs as you; the guest sees only that subtree).
  • --ssh boots the VM headless in the background and drops you straight into an SSH session (best for full-screen TUIs / truecolor). Drop it to use the serial console in the foreground instead.
  • Login user is agent, password agent. Poweroff from inside with sudo poweroff (or just exit the --ssh session).

Run bash scripts/appliance.sh --help for all flags (--no-int for no network, memory/cpu sizing, warm bases via --from/--to, etc.).

docs.title: Networking & the allowlist
docs.text:
By default the guest can only reach an allowlist of hostnames (Arch mirrors, PyPI, npm, GitHub, the Claude/Gemini/OpenAI endpoints, …). Everything else is refused by the in-guest proxy, and non-allowlisted egress never leaves the VM.

To allow more hosts, edit the regex list in proxy/mitmproxy/allowlist.py and rebuild the image (step 6). Use --no-int for a fully offline session.

docs.title: Installing an agent CLI inside the guest
docs.text:
The image ships the shell toolchain but not any agent CLI (kept generic). Inside the guest:

npm i -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code     # for example
claude        # then /login

To avoid re-installing every boot, bake it into a named warm base: --to mybase on a clean poweroff freezes the running session into a reusable base; --from mybase boots from it next time. See appliance.sh --help.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• /dev/kvm permission denied — you're not in the kvm group yet, or haven't started a fresh login session (newgrp kvm).
  • nix: command not found — open a new shell after installing nix, or source /nix/var/nix/profiles/default/etc/profile.d/nix-daemon.sh.
  • Path ... is not tracked during build — run the build from inside the git clone (not a copied/extracted tarball).
  • refusing --workspace under ... — your workspace is under $HOME; pick a dir elsewhere (see step 7).
  • Slow first boot — the appliance downloads agent-CLI deps on first use; freeze them into a warm base with --to so later boots are fast.

docs.title: Where things live
docs.text:
• Image: ~/.local/share/katastrophe/appliance-base.qcow2
  • Warm bases: ~/.local/share/katastrophe/bases/
  • Per-session runtime state: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/katastrophe/

Nothing is installed system-wide; remove the directories above and uninstall qemu/nix to fully clean up.

docs.title: kata CLI reference
docs.text:
kata is a thin Rust orchestrator over the bash scripts in scripts/. Every command has a bash scripts/<name>.sh equivalent (no extra dependencies). Build it with:

cargo build --release --manifest-path cli/Cargo.toml
export PATH="$PWD/cli/target/release:$PATH"

kata with no subcommand lists everything. Pass-through args to a sandbox go after the subcommand (e.g. kata vm --workspace DIR --ssh); -- is also accepted if a flag would otherwise be ambiguous.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Profile What it does kata doctor [--hardened] all Preflight: KVM, qemu, virtiofsd, agent user, nft, CA. --hardened also checks the bridge/nft/podman requirements. kata build-appliance appliance Build the self-contained appliance qcow2 (rootless, no host setup). kata appliance … appliance Run the rootless qemu/KVM sandbox (SLIRP + 9p, in-guest gate). kata up hardened Bring up bridge + nft gates + mitmproxy/litellm (once per boot; first run makes the CA). kata down hardened Tear down the bridge, session nft tables (not agent_isolate), and the proxies. kata build-base hardened Build the NixOS VM base qcow2 (+ the GPU container image). kata vm … hardened Run the qemu/KVM VM on the host bridge (host nft + host proxy). kata gpu … hardened Run the rootless-podman GPU sandbox. kata bases hardened List warm VM bases. kata verify hardened Check the egress gates + proxy containers are present. kata dash hardened Read-only ratatui TUI: health + egress/probe streams + nft viewer. kata disk … hardened Encrypted-USB storage: init|mount|snap|umount|reset|rollback|status. kata project … hardened Git interchange: add <repo> [name] | push <name> | pull <name> | list.

docs.title: Common sandbox flags (appliance / vm / gpu)
docs.text:
Flag Paths Meaning --workspace DIR all Host dir shared rw as /workspace (/work on GPU). Required. Refused under $HOME. --ro PATH all Extra read-only share. May live under $HOME on VM/appliance (not GPU). Repeatable (≤4 on VM/appliance). --rw PATH vm, gpu Extra read-write mount. Refused under $HOME. --no-int all No network attached. --mem SIZE / --smp N vm, appliance Guest memory / vCPUs (default 8G / 4). --from NAME vm, appliance Boot off a warm base. --to NAME vm, appliance On clean poweroff, flatten the session into a new base. --ssh vm, appliance Boot headless + auto-ssh in (truecolor PTY for TUIs). VM uses the bridge ($VM_IP); appliance forwards a localhost port. Not compatible with --no-int on the VM.

The GPU path needs no --ssh — podman run -it already gives a real PTY.

Run any script with --help (kata vm --help) for the authoritative, current flag list.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Every constant lives in config.sh, the single source of truth sourced by every script and read by the kata CLI. Don't hardcode these anywhere else. Override any value two ways:

  • Export it before invoking kata / a script: MEM=16G kata vm ….
  • Create config.local.sh next to config.sh (gitignored) — the place for machine-local secrets like AGENT_DEV_UUID. Never put those in the tracked config.sh.

docs.title: Notable knobs
docs.text:
Variable Default What it controls AGENT_UID / AGENT_USER 1001 / agent The sandbox identity. The nft skuid filter keys off the uid; the host must have this user. BRIDGE virbr-agent Host bridge for the hardened VM path. HOST_IP / VM_IP 10.201.0.1 / 10.201.0.10 Proxy IP on the bridge / the VM's static IP (also where vm --ssh connects). PROXY_PORT 8080 mitmproxy (egress gate) port. LITELLM_PORT 4000 litellm (LLM mediator) port. PROXY_CA_CERT_PUB /etc/agent-proxy/mitmproxy-ca-cert.pem World-readable public CA the GPU container mounts. AGENT_IMG agent-base GPU container image name. OCI_RUNTIME crun OCI runtime for rootless podman (runc also works). VM_BASE / VM_BASES_DIR /var/lib/agent-vm/base.qcow2 / /mnt/vm-bases Blank VM base / warm-base dir. MOUNT_HOME /mnt/agent-home Persistent agent-owned work tree (@home). Point this at any suitably-owned dir if you skip the USB layer. AGENT_STATE_DIR /mnt/agent-state Agent creds/state (@state); GPU Claude login persists here. SHARED_STORAGE_DIR /mnt/shared-storage You-rw / agent-ro inputs + git ingress (@shared). MOUNT_SNAPS /mnt/snapshots root:root pre-session snapshots (@snapshots). APPLIANCE_DIR ~/.local/share/katastrophe Appliance images + bases (rootless, XDG). APPLIANCE_SSH_PORT 2222 First localhost port appliance --ssh tries (bumps up if busy). AGENT_DEV_UUID (unset) config.local.sh only. LUKS partition UUID for the encrypted-USB storage. disk.sh errors if unset. FORBIDDEN_WORKSPACE_PREFIX your $HOME Foot-gun guard: --workspace/--rw under this are refused. Export empty to bypass.

See config.sh itself for the full, commented list — it documents the storage subvolume ownership model and the per-profile network constants inline.

docs.title: Related work
docs.text:
Informal field notes: runtimes/tools we considered and didn't pick, and how this compares to NVIDIA OpenShell (the closest larger project). The point is a 3-second "how is this different", plus a place to track ideas worth stealing later. Not exhaustive, not neutral — these are choices for this threat model (a single-user laptop, GPU sometimes needed, host root out of scope).

The two surviving sandbox shapes — rootless podman + skuid nft (gpu.sh) and qemu/KVM + virtiofs (vm.sh), plus the self-contained appliance — aren't "best in the abstract". They're the ones whose failure modes you can read straight off the kernel.

docs.title: Sandbox runtimes we rejected
docs.text:
• gVisor (runsc) — user-space syscall interception. No first-class GPU passthrough (nvproxy is experimental and lags drivers — and the NVIDIA module is the actual risk surface). Syscall overhead on compilers/installs is real. Adds a second runtime to patch. Right call for multi-tenant shared metal, not one laptop.
  • Kata Containers — a tiny VM per container. By the time you've paid for the guest kernel + init + agent + virtio-fs, you have the VM path — but with another orchestrator (containerd-shim-kata) to audit. GPU still rides the same host CDI plumbing as rootless podman, so the VM boundary doesn't shield an in-driver compromise.
  • Firecracker — no virtio-fs (block only), so sharing /workspace becomes a 9p/tarball hack; "edit on host, run in sandbox" is the whole point.
  • Cloud Hypervisor — does have virtio-fs and would be a reasonable swap-in for microVM boot speed. We stayed on qemu only because it's everywhere, KVM is the ring-0 piece we trust, and the qemu CLI is well-trodden. Revisit if boot time/RAM across many VMs ever matters.
  • bubblewrap / unshare-only — no network isolation that holds if the agent breaks seccomp or finds a kernel bug. The host-kernel skuid drop fires in ring 0 regardless of what happens inside the userns; bwrap can't offer that.
  • LXC / systemd-nspawn — nspawn defaults expose more host (DBus, journald) than wanted; locking it down just reproduces rootless podman with a less-tested rootless story.
  • Qubes-style — wrong granularity (a permanent AppVM/NetVM split of the whole desktop), and its value is not trusting host root, which this model already accepts as out of scope.
  • NVIDIA vGPU / MIG / SR-IOV — consumer GPUs have none of it; licensing is hostile even on datacenter parts. The session-scoped /dev/nvidia* ACL solves the real problem (give the agent CUDA without cold-swapping host drivers).

docs.title: Egress-gate choices
docs.text:
• nftables over iptables — atomic ruleset reloads (nft -f all-or-nothing) and one tool for v4+v6; the punch-through dance in net-up.sh is cleaner with named, commented tables.
  • skuid filtering over cgroup net_cls/BPF — the agent uid is already a stable tag (rootless podman runs the container as the host agent uid), so a skuid rule is a one-liner; no BPF program to write and pin.
  • pasta, never --network=bridge — a bridge forwards in-kernel without ever touching the user's sockets, so the skuid filter never sees the packets. Pasta keeps host-side sockets carrying skuid=1001.
  • mitmproxy over tinyproxy (the switch that defined Phase 1) — tinyproxy did SNI-layer hostname allowlisting in ~6k lines of C, but can't decrypt TLS, so it can't expose plaintext LLM messages to the probe or do L7 method/path rules. Terminating TLS was the price of having a probe at all.
  • mitmproxy over Privoxy/Squid — those are full web proxies (more code, more knobs, more ways to misconfigure the allowlist). The addon is one short Python file.

docs.title: Image build
docs.text:
• Nix flake over packer / virt-customize — packer needs SSH up first; virt-customize drags in libguestfs. The flake is ~60 lines and reproducible by construction.
  • NixOS over Debian/Ubuntu base — declarative /etc/network/interfaces ordering vs DHCP is surprising, cloud-init is another debug layer, and seeding zsh/starship/TERM across skel + systemd is busywork NixOS folds into one block. (The GPU container is the deliberate exception — Arch/FHS, because CDI bind-mounts the host driver libs into FHS paths a Nix store can't resolve.)

docs.title: vs NVIDIA OpenShell
docs.text:
OpenShell is the closest larger project — worth tracking for ideas. It's enterprise-shaped (gateway + sandbox + gRPC + persistent DB + multiple compute drivers + mTLS PKI, ~41k LoC Rust in the core crates); this is laptop-shaped (a few hundred lines of bash/Nix/Python). Different points on the complexity/value curve, different threat models — not a migrate-wholesale target.

What only this has (the reasons not to switch):

  • The persistent host-kernel agent_isolate skuid drop. OpenShell's outermost boundary is the libkrun VM / container — if that's breached, there's nothing underneath. Here, an escape that's still uid 1001 hits a ring-0 nft drop to RFC1918/tailnet/link-local regardless of the runtime. This is the single biggest delta vs every off-the-shelf sandbox.
  • OAuth subscription CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini CLI) as first-class. OpenShell's provider model is API-key only (*_API_KEY env vars); the OAuth dance isn't modelled. Here it's handled via the allowlist + per-deployment CA, accepting that OAuth creds live in the sandbox.
  • A probe that can host a Python model. It sits naturally on the mitmproxy addon + litellm callback. OpenShell's OPA Rego is a decision language, not a place to run a model — you'd be shelling out to a sidecar.
  • No persistent control plane. kata down and the host is back to "nothing running". OpenShell keeps a gateway daemon + per-sandbox supervisor + PKI — all latent surface during off-hours.

What OpenShell does better (ideas to watch / grab):

  • L7 method/path policy via a YAML schema (access: read-only|read-write, per-binary pinning). We do hostname allowlisting today; the addon runs on post-MITM plaintext, so a small subset of this schema (host + access mode) could land in proxy/mitmproxy/addon.py in ~30 lines. Borrow the schema shape, not the OPA engine.
  • Per-sandbox ephemeral CA generated per VM build rather than once per host — strictly better hygiene (a leaked CA can't decrypt another session). Mostly a tweak to where net-up.sh mints the cert.
  • Trust-on-first-use binary identity at the proxy (resolve the calling PID via /proc/net/tcp → pin the binary hash, so a compromised dep can't start using an allowlisted host). Hard for us — the proxy runs outside the VM and can't see in-guest PIDs without a sidecar. Skip unless the proxy moves in-guest.
  • Structured audit log (they use OCSF). Ours is ctx.log lines + the probe's JSONL PROBE-EVENT stream — fine for one user; revisit a real schema if tuning the allowlist from history becomes a thing.
  • SSRF / hard-blocked IMDS ranges in the proxy (169.254.169.254, RFC1918). We already get this in ring 0 via agent_isolate + agent_vm default-drop — don't borrow, ours is stronger.

What would change the calculus: going API-key-only (then their provider model + inference.local + L7 policy is a real win, and you'd mainly give up the skuid layer); the probe becoming a Rego-style ruleset instead of a model (then OPA is in scope); or wanting multi-user/team sandboxing (single-user here by construction). None true today.

docs.title: Not seriously considered
docs.text: Docker/Rancher Desktop (adds a VM to host a runtime we already have rootless), Snap/Flatpak (desktop-app shape, portals), Anbox/WSL (irrelevant), cloud enclaves (Nitro / Confidential VMs — cloud only).

docs.title: Threat model
docs.text:
What this sandbox defends against, what it accepts, and where the load-bearing controls are. Component details are in Components; this page is the inventory of threats and their status.

Reporting a vulnerability

The reporting policy + supported-versions statement live in the repository's root SECURITY.md (so GitHub's "Report a vulnerability" UI can find it). This page is the threat inventory it points at.

Legend: L likelihood · I impact · S status (accepted, mitigated, partial, open, deferred).

docs.title: The three profiles differ in where egress is enforced
docs.text:
Profile Outer wall qemu/podman runs as A runtime escape lands as Egress gate Appliance KVM (strong) you you in-guest proxy + in-guest nft (defeatable by guest root, can't escape the VM) Hardened VM KVM (strong) agent (§5d drop) agent uid host nft (agent_isolate + agent_vm), survives guest compromise GPU user namespaces (weakest) agent uid agent uid host nft (agent_isolate + agent_uid_egress)

Guest-root inside any VM is not an escape — it stays trapped behind KVM and touches nothing on the host. The "lands as" column is where a real hypervisor or namespace escape would put the attacker. Weak wall ↔ punishing host gate; strong wall ↔ the gate can be softer. The appliance trades the host gate away for zero host setup, so its allowlist is only as strong as the guest kernel.

Every threat below maps to a runnable check on the Security invariants page — read this for the why, run those for the proof.

docs.title: Trust boundaries
docs.text:
1. Agent uid (1001) — load-bearing for the GPU and hardened-VM paths. Kernel DAC, the agent_uid_egress skuid filter, the persistent agent_isolate skuid chain, and /dev/nvidia* ACL gating all hang on this uid. Anything crossing uid 1001 → another uid on the host (including root) breaches the sandbox. Since §5d, qemu in vm.sh also drops to this uid, so a hypervisor escape lands here too (instead of root).
  2. The host bridge — load-bearing for the hardened-VM path. The VM lives behind KVM; the bridge is the only host-exposed surface. The agent_vm table filters everything off the bridge.
  3. Host root / the host user. Out of scope — both can disable every control.
  4. The appliance has no host trust boundary beyond KVM itself: the proxy, the allowlist and the nft drop all live inside the disposable guest. This is the deliberate cost of needing no host setup.

docs.title: K.1 Kernel CVE reachable from userns (GPU path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / critical / accepted
  • kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1 is required for rootless podman. Historically vulnerable (CVE-2022-0185, CVE-2022-2602).
  • Mitigation: a hardened kernel + prompt kernel updates.

docs.title: K.2 NVIDIA driver CVE (GPU path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / critical / accepted
  • Proprietary module, large kernel surface, recurring priv-esc CVEs. Reachable from the agent uid via the session-scoped /dev/nvidia* ACL.
  • Mitigation: track driver CVEs and update promptly. The ACL is removed on session exit (kata disk umount / the GPU run's cleanup), but a compromise during a session lands in ring 0 instantly.

docs.title: K.3 sudo CVE
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / critical / accepted
  • vm.sh, net-up.sh and disk.sh run via sudo. A sudo bug lands host root.
  • Mitigation: keep sudo patched.

docs.title: K.4 Persistent agent_isolate chain is load-bearing
docs.text:
• L/I/S: n/a / n/a / do not remove
  • Installed to /etc/nftables.d/ (or /etc/nftables.conf), agent_isolate drops skuid 1001 → RFC1918 + CGNAT (tailnet) + link-local + multicast. The GPU and hardened-VM paths rely on it for LAN / VPN / other-host isolation.
  • Removing it re-exposes private services to the agent. net-up.sh refuses to start if it isn't loaded. The appliance does not use it (no host nft) — its equivalent drop runs inside the guest.

docs.title: K.5 KVM / qemu escape (VM + appliance paths)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / critical / accepted
  • KVM and qemu device emulation have had escapes (e.g. CVE-2019-14378). No PCIe passthrough in these builds — the emulated surface is virtio-net, virtio-blk, and vhost-user-fs (virtiofsd) / virtio-9p (appliance).
  • Mitigation: keep qemu patched; a hardened kernel reduces ring-0 surface. Since §5d the hardened VM runs qemu under -run-with user=agent, so an escape lands as the agent uid (caught by agent_isolate) rather than root. The appliance runs qemu as you, so an escape there lands as you — acceptable because that profile is for trying-it-out / no-API-key work, and a real escape past KVM already has your uid's reach.

docs.title: K.6 virtiofsd compromise (hardened VM path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / high / partial
  • virtiofsd runs as root on the host and serves the VM's /workspace (and any --rw/--ro mounts). A guest-side exploit against virtiofsd lands as host root — and unlike qemu (§5d), virtiofsd still needs root to traverse the shared trees, so it can't be dropped the same way.
  • Mitigation: --sandbox=chroot is set; upstream Rust virtiofsd has no published escapes to date. Limit --rw mounts to data the agent needs; prefer --ro ingress (the read-only path is the intended way to feed code in).
  • Open: no seccomp profile pinned for virtiofsd in this repo. (The appliance uses virtio-9p served by qemu-as-you, not root virtiofsd, so this threat does not apply there.)

docs.title: K.7 Bridge / nftables misconfig (hardened VM path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / high / mitigated
  • The VM reaches the internet only via the proxy. If agent_vm is missing or reordered, the VM could reach the host directly.
  • Mitigation: vm.sh preflights nft list table inet agent_vm and the bridge link, and refuses to start if either is missing. net-down.sh is idempotent — re-running net-up.sh resets the rules to a known state. kata verify reports the gate state.

docs.title: K.7b Proxy container hardening (mitmproxy / litellm)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / med / mitigated
  • Both proxy containers run rootful podman, --network=host bound only to the RFC1918 proxy IP, --cap-drop=ALL, --security-opt=no-new-privileges, known upstream images, --read-only (litellm), under the default podman AppArmor profile.
  • Do not re-add apparmor=unconfined. An earlier revision unconfined both containers on a wrong diagnosis. The real startup failure was --cap-drop=ALL colliding with the mitmproxy image's root docker-entrypoint (a stat needing CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH, then a su-exec needing CAP_SETUID/SETGID). The fix is --user 1000:1000 --entrypoint mitmdump, which bypasses the wrapper; both containers then run clean under the default profile.

docs.title: K.8 mitmproxy allowlist drift
docs.text:
• L/I/S: med / med / accepted
  • Adding a host like gist.github.com opens a generic exfil channel. PyPI's publish endpoint, HF model-uploads, and GitHub gists are exfil-by-design.
  • Mitigation: keep the allowlist minimal; review proxy/mitmproxy/allowlist.py before appending.

docs.title: K.8b mitmproxy as a TLS-terminating CA (hardened path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / high / partial
  • The hardened mitmproxy holds a per-deployment CA private key (mode 0600) that the VM trusts. Anyone who can read that key can mint valid leaf certs for any allowlisted host as far as the VM is concerned.
  • Mitigation: per-deployment CA (not shared across hosts); the private key never leaves the host (only the public cert is published world-readable for the GPU container and baked into the VM trust store). The addon must never log request bodies. (The appliance does TLS passthrough, not termination, so it holds no CA — it can't read HTTPS bodies, only SNI/Host for allowlisting.)
  • Open: no automated CA rotation. Rotate by clearing the CA dir and re-running kata up (+ kata build-base to re-bake the VM trust store).

docs.title: K.8c DNS / raw-socket exfil (both host-gated paths)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / high / mitigated
  • The proxy + probe only see HTTP(S) they intercept. Direct UDP/53 to a public resolver (DNS tunnelling) or raw/AF_PACKET frames would be channels they're blind to.
  • Mitigation: the agent needs no direct DNS — it reaches the proxy by IP. GPU: the terminal reject in agent_uid_egress drops all non-proxy egress including UDP/53. VM: agent_vm forward is policy drop, so the guest's direct egress is never routed. Verify from inside with scripts/audit-egress.sh (T1/T2).
  • Open: AF_PACKET reachability is argued safe (GPU uses pasta userspace networking; the VM bridge is NAT-routed, not L2-bridged to the wire) but not yet runtime-verified — audit-egress.sh T3 checks it; add a seccomp AF_PACKET block to gpu.sh if it turns out reachable.

docs.title: K.9 Storage persistence
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / med / accepted
  • The agent's work tree (@home) persists between sessions, so an implant survives. By design — real work needs persistence.
  • Mitigation: kata disk mount takes a read-only pre-session snapshot of @home/@state into the root:root @snapshots subvol (out of the agent's reach), kata disk snap takes an on-demand one before a risky run, and kata disk rollback restores one. Snapshots live on a subvolume the agent cannot enter, list, or btrfs subvolume delete.

docs.title: K.9b VM-base / snapshot poisoning (hardened path)
docs.text:
• L/I/S: low / critical / mitigated
  • vm.sh parses base qcow2s, and qcow2 parsing is a code-execution surface. An agent-writable base or snapshot would be a host-code-exec path without a hypervisor escape.
  • Mitigation: @bases and @snapshots are you-/root-owned subvolumes with no agent write access — siblings of @home, never nested under it. The only writer of @bases is vm.sh --to, which runs as root. No agent-uid path writes a root-parsed base (verified, §5a).

docs.title: K.10 Compromised host user account
docs.text: • Out of scope. sudo access collapses the whole model.

docs.title: K.11 Physical access / firmware
docs.text: • Out of scope. Full-disk encryption (if you use the encrypted-USB storage layer) protects powered-off devices only.

docs.title: K.12 Side channels (Spectre / Meltdown / MDS / TAA)
docs.text: • Out of scope. Default kernel mitigations are assumed on; full side-channel isolation would need CPU pinning + SMT off, which this repo does not do.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• "Accepted" = an intentional trade-off given this threat model.
  • The paths are independent boundaries — escaping one doesn't bypass another. They share only the allowlist (K.8), the hardened CA (K.8b), and the kernel's CVE exposure (K.1, K.5).

docs.title: Testing & verification
docs.text:
This section is a hands-on walkthrough: build each piece, run it, and prove the invariant it's supposed to uphold. It doubles as a tutorial (the happy path) and a security checklist (the adversarial path). Run it after first setup, and again after any kernel / qemu / podman / driver upgrade.

Every check below states a command and the expected result. Where a check proves a security property, it's tagged with the threat it backs (e.g. K.8c) — cross-reference the Threat model.

docs.title: How to read a test
docs.text:
What it checks · Run the command · Expect the outcome · (Proves the invariant, when security-relevant).

A test that "passes" by failing loudly (a refused connection, a 403, a denied syscall) is the common shape here — the gate doing its job looks like something not working.

docs.title: Test matrix
docs.text: Area Page What you prove Appliance profile Appliance builds, boots, --ssh, in-guest allowlist, offline mode, warm bases Hardened VM Hardened VM bridge + nft gate, --ssh, qemu drops to agent (§5d), bases out of reach GPU container GPU rootless podman egress pin, CA trust, Claude login persists, raw-socket denial Egress & probe Egress & probe allowlist allow/deny, litellm key-swap, probe pre/post, fail-closed Security invariants Security invariants the §5 hardening + threat-model claims, each as a runnable check

docs.title: The one tool to know: audit-egress.sh
docs.text:
scripts/audit-egress.sh runs inside the sandbox (VM or GPU container) and proves four properties from the agent's own vantage point:

Test Expect T1 DNS to a public resolver (8.8.8.8:53) blocked (no UDP/53 exfil) T2 direct TCP to a public IP (1.1.1.1:443, no proxy) blocked (egress is proxy-only) T3 create a raw / AF_PACKET socket denied (no L2/raw exfil) T4 reach the proxy (pypi.org via the gate) OK — positive control

It exits 0 iff all four land the expected way. T4 is the positive control: it stops T1–T3 from "passing" simply because the network is dead. It's the single most important test in this section — most security-invariant checks reduce to "run audit-egress.sh in profile X and read T1–T4".

# inside the VM or container (it's on the shared workspace, or scp/copy it in):
bash audit-egress.sh

Start with the profile you set up — Appliance if you followed the default install, Hardened VM / GPU otherwise.

docs.title: Testing the appliance
docs.text: The appliance is the zero-host-setup profile: a throwaway qemu/KVM VM with the egress allowlist enforced inside the guest (in-guest mitmproxy on 127.0.0.1:8080 + in-guest nftables pinning uid 1001 to it). No root, no bridge, no containers. See the install guide to build it.

docs.title: 1. Host preflight
docs.text:
What KVM + qemu are usable · Run · Expect all green.

kata doctor

docs.title: 2. Build & boot
docs.text:
What the image builds and the guest boots to a shell.

kata build-appliance                      # first build is slow (Nix pulls a lot)
mkdir -p /tmp/proj
kata appliance --workspace /tmp/proj      # serial console, foreground

Expect a fastfetch banner, a [vm] prompt tag, and /workspace containing whatever is in /tmp/proj. sudo poweroff to exit.

docs.title: 3. --ssh ergonomics
docs.text:
What headless boot + auto-ssh gives a proper truecolor PTY (for nvim/TUIs).

kata appliance --workspace /tmp/proj --ssh

Expect booting headless, waiting for ssh …, then you land in an ssh shell (agent@127.0.0.1, a forwarded localhost port). Open nvim — colours and redraw should be crisp (unlike the serial console). Exit the shell → the guest powers off cleanly.

docs.title: 4. In-guest allowlist — allow vs deny
docs.text:
What the in-guest proxy permits allowlisted hosts and refuses the rest. Proves the egress allowlist (in-guest enforcement).

Inside the guest (the HTTP(S)_PROXY env already points at 127.0.0.1:8080):

curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/simple/   # → 200
curl -sS https://example.com/                                        # → 403 (refused)

Expect 200 for the allowlisted host, a 403 allowlist denial for example.com.

docs.title: 5. Egress audit from inside
docs.text:
What DNS/direct-TCP/raw-socket exfil are all closed; the proxy works. Proves T1–T4 (see the overview). K.8c (DNS/raw).

The audit script defaults to the hardened proxy IP, so point it at the appliance's in-guest proxy:

PROXY_IP=127.0.0.1 bash audit-egress.sh

Expect 4 passed, 0 failed:

  • T1 no DNS reply (in-guest nft drops uid-1001 UDP/53),
  • T2 direct TCP to 1.1.1.1:443 refused,
  • T3 raw/AF_PACKET denied,
  • T4 proxy returns 2xx/3xx.

docs.title: 6. Offline mode
docs.text:
What --no-int detaches the network entirely.

kata appliance --workspace /tmp/proj --no-int
# inside:
curl -sS --max-time 4 https://pypi.org/ ; echo "rc=$?"     # non-zero, no egress

Expect the curl to fail — there is no NIC at all.

docs.title: 7. Warm bases (--to / --from)
docs.text:
What a clean session can be frozen into a reusable base and booted back.

# bake: install something, then poweroff cleanly INSIDE the guest
kata appliance --workspace /tmp/proj --to mytools --ssh
#   (in guest) npm i -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code ; exit   → bakes on clean exit

kata bases                                # lists mytools
kata appliance --workspace /tmp/proj --from mytools --ssh
#   (in guest) claude --version           → already installed

Expect --to to print saved base: …/mytools.qcow2 only on a clean poweroff (a Ctrl-C/kill leaves QEMU_CLEAN=0 and bakes nothing), and --from to boot with your tooling present.

Credentials in bases

--to after an OAuth login bakes those creds into the base image. Keep a cred-free base separate from a logged-in one.

docs.title: 8. (Negative) the appliance allowlist is not a host boundary
docs.text:
What confirms the documented trade-off, not a bug.

Inside the guest, sudo nft flush ruleset (you have passwordless sudo) lets the agent reach the SLIRP gateway directly — the in-guest allowlist is defeatable by guest root. That's expected: the appliance's wall is KVM, not the in-guest gate. A guest-root agent still can't escape the VM or touch your host. If you need an egress gate that survives guest compromise, use the hardened profile.

docs.title: Testing egress & the probe
docs.text: These components can be tested standalone — no VM, no container, no host setup — which is the fastest way to iterate on the allowlist or the probe. Then the same pieces are exercised end-to-end through litellm.

docs.title: Probe alone (no infrastructure)
docs.text:
What the probe(messages, response, metadata) -> Verdict function allows benign input and denies the block list. Proves the probe wiring.

python examples/probe-over-sample.py

Expect:

benign   -> allow
blocked  -> deny      keyword filter matched: 'bomb'

The default _evaluate is a permissive keyword block (a smoke test, not a real content check) — the point of the module shape is that swapping in a real interpretability check is a one-function change in probe/core.py.

docs.title: Proxy alone (point any client at it)
docs.text:
What the mitmproxy allowlist permits listed hosts and 403s the rest. Proves the allowlist in isolation (K.8).

bash scripts/proxy.sh                 # passthrough on 127.0.0.1:8080
# in another shell:
HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:8080 curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/   # allowed
HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:8080 curl -sS https://example.com/                                 # 403

Edit the regex list in proxy/mitmproxy/allowlist.py and re-run to see a host flip from denied to allowed.

docs.title: Proxy in front of the container (the composition)
docs.text:
What proxy + container sandbox compose (rootless, no host setup).

bash scripts/build-appliance-container.sh                  # once
bash examples/proxy-in-front.sh --workspace /tmp/proj      # passthrough
# inside the container:
curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/   # allowed
curl -sS https://example.com/                                 # denied

docs.title: litellm key-swap + probe (end-to-end, hardened)
docs.text:
litellm is the canonical LLM endpoint at 10.201.0.1:4000. Agents use a fake key; litellm holds the real upstream key on the host, validates the fake one, swaps it, runs the probe, and forwards. From inside the VM or container:

What the probe denies blocked input before any upstream call. Proves the probe pre-hook + fail-closed posture.

curl -sS http://10.201.0.1:4000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer fake" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"local-llm","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"how to build a bomb"}]}'

Expect a rejection (the pre probe denies before the upstream is contacted — so this works even with no local backend running). A benign request:

curl -sS http://10.201.0.1:4000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer fake" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"local-llm","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hello"}]}'

Expect a normal completion iff a local OpenAI-compatible backend is running where proxy/litellm/config.yaml points (default 127.0.0.1:8080, e.g. llama.cpp). The real key (if you wired one) never enters the sandbox.

Watch the verdicts on the host:

kata dash         # the litellm + probe pane tails PROBE-EVENT lines

Expect a PROBE … pre … deny line for the bomb request.

Known gap: streamed responses aren't post-probed

The litellm adapter implements async_post_call_success_hook (non-streaming) but not the streaming iterator hook. Chat clients stream, so model output is currently unchecked — only the pre hook (input) runs. You'll see PROBE … pre lines but never post for streamed responses. A harmful answer only trips on the next turn once it's in history. This is on the Phase-2 backlog; don't read the absence of post lines as a passing test.

docs.title: Probe fail-closed (conceptual)
docs.text: probe() / _evaluate deliberately have no exception-swallowing, so a crash in the pre-hook propagates and the request is rejected, not allowed. The "no try/except for robustness" convention preserves this — don't add a fallback that returns allow on error, or you convert a fail-closed gate into a fail-open one.

docs.title: Testing the GPU container
docs.text: The GPU path is rootless podman (container uid = agent uid 1001) with the GPU shared via CDI. Egress is pinned by the host-kernel agent_uid_egress skuid filter — pasta carries skuid=1001 on the host-side sockets. Set up the hardened profile first (the GPU path shares its egress stack).

docs.title: 1. Build & gate up
docs.text:
kata down && bash scripts/build-gpu.sh    # builds in agent's rootless store; gate DOWN
kata up && kata verify                     # agent_uid_egress present

Build with the gate down

kata up pins uid 1001 to the proxy, so pacman/npm in the build hit mirror timeouts. Build with kata down, then kata up to run. The build script preflights this.

docs.title: 2. Run it
docs.text:
What the container starts with a proper PTY and the GPU visible.

kata gpu --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj
# inside:
nvidia-smi                                 # GPU listed

Expect a [container] prompt, truecolor (no --ssh needed — podman run -it is already a real PTY), /work = your workspace, and nvidia-smi showing the card. The session-scoped /dev/nvidia* ACL is removed on exit.

docs.title: 3. Allowlist + CA trust
docs.text:
What the container trusts the mounted public CA and the allowlist applies. Proves the GPU-path CA gotcha (allowed-HTTPS must verify).

Inside the container:

curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/simple/   # → 200, no TLS error
curl -sS https://example.com/                                        # → 403

Expect 200 with no certificate error — net-up.sh publishes the public CA world-readable and gpu.sh mounts it at /etc/agent-ca.pem, with NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS/SSL_CERT_FILE/REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE/CURL_CA_BUNDLE/ PIP_CERT all pointed at it. A cert error means the mount/env is missing.

docs.title: 4. Egress audit from inside
docs.text:
What DNS/direct-TCP/raw-socket closed; proxy works. Proves T1–T4. K.8c.

bash /work/audit-egress.sh        # or wherever it's mounted; default PROXY_IP is correct

Expect 4 passed, 0 failed. T1/T2 are closed by the terminal reject in agent_uid_egress (only :8080/:4000 to 10.201.0.1 are exempt).

T3 is the one to watch on this path

If T3 reports AF_PACKET:CREATED (a raw socket could be created), verify frames can't actually leave — and if they can, add a seccomp profile blocking AF_PACKET to gpu.sh. This is the open item in threat K.8c. pasta is userspace networking (no host-wire L2), so it should be denied, but this path is the least-walled, so confirm it at runtime.

docs.title: 5. Egress is pinned at the host kernel (skuid)
docs.text:
What the gate survives a container-side bypass attempt — it's the host nft skuid drop, not a container env var. Proves trust-boundary 1 / K.4.

Inside the container, unset the proxy env and try direct egress:

HTTP_PROXY= HTTPS_PROXY= curl -sS --max-time 4 https://1.1.1.1/ ; echo "rc=$?"

Expect failure — the host agent_uid_egress rejects uid-1001 traffic to anything but the proxy regardless of what the container sets. (Contrast the appliance, where the gate is in-guest and is defeatable.)

docs.title: 6. Claude Code login persists across --read-only
docs.text:
What OAuth creds survive even though the container home is read-only. Proves the category-1 credential model + the read-only-home fix.

# inside:
claude        # then /login, complete the OAuth flow through the allowlist
exit
kata gpu --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj
claude        # still logged in

Expect the second run to be already authenticated — ~/.claude and ~/.claude.json are bind-mounted from agent-owned @state on the encrypted USB, so the write survives the --read-only rootfs (which would otherwise silently swallow it → "Login successful" then "Not logged in").

docs.title: Testing the hardened VM
docs.text: The hardened VM runs qemu/KVM on a host bridge, with egress enforced in the host kernel (the agent_isolate skuid drop + the agent_vm table) and a host-side TLS-terminating mitmproxy. Set it up via the hardened guide first.

docs.title: 1. Gate is up
docs.text:
What both nft tables and both proxy containers are present. Proves K.4 / K.7.

kata up
kata verify

Expect green for agent_isolate, agent_uid_egress, agent_vm, mitmproxy, and litellm.

docs.title: 2. Boot the VM (and --ssh)
docs.text:
What the VM boots on the bridge; --ssh gives a truecolor PTY.

# foreground serial console:
kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj

# headless + auto-ssh over the bridge (better for nvim/TUIs):
kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj --ssh

Expect with --ssh: booting headless, waiting for ssh on 10.201.0.10:22 …, then an ssh shell as agent@10.201.0.10. Exit → the guest powers off cleanly (and bakes a --to base if you passed one).

--ssh needs the bridge

It reaches the guest at the static $VM_IP, so it's incompatible with --no-int (no NIC). Host→VM:22 works because the agent_vm input chain accepts host-initiated established,related return traffic — NEW from the VM is still dropped, so this doesn't widen egress.

docs.title: 3. In-guest allowlist — allow vs deny
docs.text:
What the host mitmproxy permits allowlisted hosts and refuses the rest; the VM trusts the per-deployment CA (TLS termination, not passthrough). Proves the egress allowlist + CA trust (K.8 / K.8b).

Inside the guest:

curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://pypi.org/simple/   # → 200
curl -sS https://example.com/                                        # → 403

Expect 200 allowed, 403 denied, with no TLS error — the VM's trust store contains the mitmproxy CA. A cert error here means the CA wasn't baked in (rebuild with kata build-base).

docs.title: 4. Egress audit from inside
docs.text:
What DNS/direct-TCP/raw-socket exfil closed; proxy works. Proves T1–T4. K.8c.

# inside the VM (it's on the shared workspace):
bash audit-egress.sh        # default PROXY_IP=10.201.0.1 is correct here

Expect 4 passed, 0 failed. T1 relies on agent_vm forward being policy drop (the guest's UDP/53 is never routed); T2 on the same; T4 confirms the proxy path is alive.

docs.title: 5. qemu dropped to the agent uid (§5d)
docs.text:
What a hypervisor escape would land as agent, not root. Proves the §5d drop / trust-boundary 1.

While the VM is running, on the host:

ps -o pid,user,comm -C qemu-system-x86_64

Expect the qemu process owned by agent (not root). The parent vm.sh stays root for cleanup, but the vCPU process — the thing an escape would ride — runs as uid 1001, caught by agent_isolate. If you see root, your qemu is < 8.0 (no -run-with user=) and refused to drop unless ALLOW_QEMU_ROOT=1.

docs.title: 6. Bases are out of agent reach (§5a / K.9b)
docs.text:
What the agent can't write a VM base (qcow2 parsing is a root-code-exec surface). Proves K.9b.

sudo -u agent touch /mnt/vm-bases/evil.qcow2     # → Permission denied
sudo -u agent ls /mnt/snapshots                  # → Permission denied

Expect both to be refused — @bases is you-owned 700, @snapshots is root:root 700, both siblings of @home, never nested under it.

docs.title: 7. Read-only ingress works, read-write home is guarded
docs.text:
What --ro of a real project under your home is allowed; --workspace under your home is refused. Proves the foot-gun guard (§3 relaxation).

kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/myproj --ro ~/code/somelib   # OK
kata vm --workspace ~/code/myproj                                     # refused

Expect the --ro mount to appear at /mnt/ro_1 in the guest; the --workspace under $HOME to be refused early with refusing --workspace under ….

docs.title: 8. Live monitor (optional)
docs.text:
sudo -v && kata dash

Expect the overview pane to show the running VM's base · ip · mem · overlay, read from VM_STATE_FILE, plus live mitmproxy/litellm/nft panes.

docs.title: Security invariants
docs.text: A consolidated checklist: every load-bearing claim in the threat model, paired with a command that proves it and the expected outcome. Run this after first setup and after any kernel / qemu / podman / driver upgrade. ✗ in "Expect" means the action must fail — the gate doing its job looks like something not working.

docs.title: Egress is closed (run audit-egress.sh inside the sandbox)
docs.text:
# Invariant Threat Run (inside sandbox) Expect T1 No DNS exfil to public resolvers K.8c audit-egress.sh ✗ no DNS reply T2 No direct TCP (egress is proxy-only) K.8c audit-egress.sh ✗ connection refused T3 No raw / AF_PACKET sockets K.8c audit-egress.sh ✗ socket denied T4 The proxy is reachable (positive control) — audit-egress.sh ✓ HTTP 2xx/3xx

One command covers all four: bash audit-egress.sh (use PROXY_IP=127.0.0.1 in the appliance). It must print 4 passed, 0 failed.

T3 on the GPU path

If T3 reports AF_PACKET:CREATED, confirm frames can't actually leave and add a seccomp AF_PACKET block to gpu.sh if they can. Open item in K.8c.

docs.title: The host gate survives a sandbox bypass (hardened only)
docs.text: Invariant Threat Run Expect Egress pin is host-kernel, not container env K.4 inside: HTTP_PROXY= HTTPS_PROXY= curl --max-time 4 https://1.1.1.1/ ✗ fails anyway agent_isolate is loaded persistently K.4 host: sudo nft list table inet agent_isolate ✓ present net-up.sh refuses without it K.4 host: unload it, run kata up ✗ refuses to start kata verify reports gates + proxies K.7 host: kata verify ✓ all green

docs.title: Escape lands soft, not as root (§5d)
docs.text:
Invariant Threat Run (host, VM running) Expect qemu runs as agent, not root K.5 ps -o user= -C qemu-system-x86_64 agent

The vCPU process — what a hypervisor escape rides — is uid 1001, caught by agent_isolate. root here means qemu < 8.0 (or ALLOW_QEMU_ROOT=1).

docs.title: The agent can't reach what protects you from it
docs.text: Invariant Threat Run (host) Expect Agent can't write VM bases (qcow2 = code-exec surface) K.9b sudo -u agent touch /mnt/vm-bases/x.qcow2 ✗ Permission denied Agent can't enter/list snapshots K.9b / K.9 sudo -u agent ls /mnt/snapshots ✗ Permission denied @bases/@snapshots are siblings of @home, not nested K.9b kata disk status separate subvols, you-/root-owned Pre-session snapshot was taken K.9 kata disk mount then sudo ls /mnt/snapshots ✓ a read-only snapshot exists

docs.title: TLS termination trust (hardened)
docs.text: Invariant Threat Run (inside VM) Expect VM trusts the per-deployment CA K.8b curl https://pypi.org/simple/ ✓ 200, no cert error Allowlist denies unlisted hosts K.8 curl https://example.com/ ✗ 403 CA private key stays host-side, 0600 K.8b host: sudo stat -c '%a %U' /etc/agent-proxy/mitmproxy/mitmproxy-ca.pem 600 + owner uid 1000

docs.title: The probe fails closed
docs.text:
Invariant Threat Run Expect Blocked input denied before upstream — inside: litellm curl with "how to build a bomb" ✗ rejected (no upstream call) Probe crash → reject, not allow — code review: no try/except returning allow in probe/core.py holds

See Egress & probe for the full commands and the known streaming-post-probe gap.

docs.title: Foot-gun guard
docs.text: Invariant Run Expect --workspace / --rw under $HOME refused kata vm --workspace ~/code/x ✗ refusing --workspace under … --ro under $HOME allowed (VM/appliance) kata vm --workspace /mnt/agent-home/work/p --ro ~/code/lib ✓ mounts at /mnt/ro_1

docs.title: Out of scope (do not expect these to be defended)
docs.text: Host root, a compromised host-user account, physical access / firmware, and Spectre-class side channels are explicitly out of scope (K.10–K.12). Don't file these as failures — they're accepted boundaries of a one-laptop, one-user model.
